text: The surgical field shows no visible peritoneal defect in the area being examined.
The surgeon maintains a preperitoneal approach during the hernia procedure, ensuring the surgical field remains outside the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum, hugging it closely to separate it from the myopectineal orifice, ensuring no indirect hernia sac is present.
The surgeon continues to dissect the myopectineal orifice (MPO), performing lateral dissection to separate tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon parietalizes the chord structures, carefully lifting the peritoneum off them, and continues this action towards the vas deferens.
The surgeon searches for the lipoma within the surgical field, examining the surrounding tissue for its identification.
The surgeon continues the dissection, focusing on separating the chord structures from the surrounding tissue and lifting the peritoneum away.
The surgeon carefully sweeps the spermatic cord away from the surrounding tissue, creating space for further dissection.
The surgeon identifies the lipoma within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies attachments to the nodal tissue located posteriorly during the dissection.
The surgeon observes the absence of further fatty tissue entering the inguinal canal, indicating a clear surgical field.
The surgeon ensures hemostasis while visually inspecting the nerves in the surgical field.
The surgeon ensures the pocket is adequately sized to allow the mesh to fully unfold without clamshelling or folding.
The surgeon places the mesh into the prepared pocket, ensuring it unfolds completely without clamshelling or folding.
The surgeon observes the flap extending beyond the area where the mesh is positioned, ensuring adequate coverage and placement.
The surgeon carefully positions the mesh within the surgical pocket, ensuring it remains straight and unfolded, avoiding any clamshelling or folding.
The surgeon proceeds to close the flap over the mesh, ensuring it extends beyond the mesh placement area.
The surgeon identifies a small defect and uses the remnant of a V-lock suture to close it.
A 14-gauge angiocatheter is used to evacuate gas from the surgical flap, ensuring the mesh remains closely adhered to the abdominal wall during desufflation.
